text: I sir, this method create nano copper powder. How about nano copper liquid ? Because, i put sample in several minutes, it have precipitation

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Nguyễn Quốc Thuận nanoparticles aggregate whenever they can - you will get a degree of aggregation - the faster you do the reaction the more aggregation you get as the ascorbic acid also helps to stabilize the particles but speed of reaction will lead to particle growth and the resultant copper dropping out as the particles get too big. If you do it at the right speed you will end up with copper nano fluid. This is experimentation on your part I am afraid

author: @74Persia
text: Hello ,I guess  hana color kind of orange brown color thats were to stop then extract the particles for ink .the a amount of cuNP in the solution is small and kind of over cooked to cuO NP  my guess on why it doesn't conduct .hana color

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: fair enough

replies.author: @74Persia
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith  I tested it .I was wrong the hana color is copper NP but its not stable .after oxidation its stable .seemingly the oxygen bond keeps the particles in the solution. so your ink is welldone not overcooked. but there a room to rise the ph level a bit to speed up the process .I added pvp and hydrazine and its conductive.  I have used silver nitrate printing over ascorbic acid successfully in the past but I just tried it with copper sulfate it prints dark brown which fades to orange then yellow and totally disappears in a few second after printing .lol any suggestion? thankyou

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
74Persia
no - not really sorry

replies.author: @74Persia
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
 The above problem is seemingly related to ascorbic acid acting as oxidizer in high concentration and presence of water and metal ion it creates sulfuric acid CuO(dark brown) then Cu+oxidize AA+remaining copper-sulfate (green>>yellow) but as the water vaporize sulfuric acid concentration will cause the reaction to go backward creating copper sulfate out of the Cu which was reduced by AA  .which is colorless at that concentration.more basic environment prevent AA to act as oxidizer..but then again this double action of AA is keeping the particle sizes at nano scale.more over its important that the core of particles do not oxidize so they heat very slowly other wise you might get CuO particles (with Cu shell around them?)they heat exactly at beak a specific bond because AA have at minimum of 3 different states. .I got under 1g of product out of that solution how much have you got ?no need for photo initiator for curing ? Im following your  research I dont know which one is the latest or if you sell any copper nano ink?. Thank you

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
74Persia
no i don't sell this stuff mate - it's meant to encourage others to experiment

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
ahmed awaad
I assume you mean a large sheet in xy direction but a few nanometers thick in the z direction? - If so that's not that difficult to do - have a look at SILAR as a technique or you could sputter it in a vacuum  -but these techniques are relatively well known and metal films of that order are relatively well understood.

author: @ssercchemistry7545
text:
Hi Robert,
just discovered your work. Looks interesting. 
I'm trying to replicate the copper nanoparticles for use in school (probably project work).
I've followed instructions (though the lab here has copper chloride so I didn't need to synthesize it).
I have repeated the mwave/swirl 60x now and am still at the reddish brown you seem to have on 9 (maybe a bit darker). There is still white solid in the bottom as well.
Any ideas?

author: @tommasopetrella9278
text: I think the white precipitate is actually copper (I) chloride, but I could be wrong.  If so then I have found what I am looking for - thanks!  Good luck on testing.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: that's cool - thanks.

author: @JustinHallPlus
text: Another method for making copper chloride is to make a mixture of HCl and dilute hydrogen peroxide (the 3% kind, not the high concentration stuff) and then just add copper.  Copper doesn't react with HCl very quickly normally, but in the presence of even a little H2O2 it will dissolve metallic copper pretty quickly.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hiya Ricardo. If you try it and have success let me know. cheers

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It most certainly would and there are some great silver based inks out there. But, I am kind of looking for a much cheaper method and a useable copper ink is the challenge. Well, a usable cheap ink. I have been playing with graphemes intercalation compounds lately I have had good results on conductivity - almost as good as metal -bit difficult to get it in an ink form though I plan to do a video on it soon

author: @zeazydice5772
text:
Whaf if you'd mix it with the carbon nanotubes you've made earlier? wouldn't it add conductivity? 

I've also read (or seen) somewhere that these kind of conductive inks have Carbon nano particles + slver nanoparticles..! Maybe you could use the same method but instead of copper, silver! :)

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: it was a bit of a combination of these two - 'microwave assisted low temperature growth of thin films in solution - Reeja-Jayan et al, scientific reports DOI:10.1038/srep01003 and synthesis of magnetite nanoparticles by microwave irradiation and characterisation by Holland and Yamaura from the seventh international latin american conference on powder technology. Hope this helps

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: though you would probably have to wash the resultant copper with 1M HCL first to get rid of the copper oxide. Another interesting thing i came across is that 'flashing' the resultant copper particles with a xenon light - like from a photoflash - reduces the copper oxide to copper - now that one is weird. I'd be very interested if you tried that one.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: That's interesting. The best way to prevent oxidation is to perform the reduction in the presence of a capping agent. Ascorbic acid both reduces and caps the copper - incidentally as does green tea - I have heard of a method using oxalic acid as the capping agent in the presence of pvp as a dispersant and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent. the one i show uses ascorbic acid as both reductant and capping agent. I suppose an interesting way might be to reduce with iron then cap with ascorbic acid

author: @deltaxcd
text:
I think particle size is least concern now, because primary objective is to prevent oxidation of copper.

From what i found if you perform reduction under high ph values copper gets reduced to Cu2O which contaminates everything.
you must perform reduction in acidic environment to get clean copper.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: They are indeed - which is part of what drove me down this route. However control of the size of the particle is qn important issue here and the microwave route helps prevent nucleation and so smaller particles. Some of the easiest ways i know of to produce copper nanoparticles is to put iron powder in copper sulphate, another is the fast electrodeposition of coper on a steel surface. However, the particles are pretty big and size is uncontrollable.

author: @deltaxcd
text:
I am also working on copper reduction, and my best result is copper sulphate + citric acid + ascorbic acid.

I would like to replace ascorbic acid with some stronger reducing agent like formaldehyde or even hypophosphite but these things are hard  get now.
